authoritative_sources.title: Occupancy Requirements of Subsidized Multifamily Housing Programs (4350.3)
authoritative_sources.publisher: U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: HUD is the federal agency for subsidized multifamily housing programs; Handbook 4350.3 is HUD's public operating handbook for eligibility, tenant selection, income/rent calculation, leasing, recertification, EIV, and tenant-file evidence.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: HUD-assisted tenant intake, eligibility, tenant selection, income and rent calculation, lease activity, recertification, EIV review, and tenant-file evidence management

authoritative_sources.title: 24 CFR 92.252 - Qualification as affordable housing: Rental housing
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: HUD's HOME rental housing rule is authoritative for HOME-assisted rental affordability qualification, rent limits, utility allowances, period of affordability, tenant income, fixed/floating units, owner reporting, and rent approval workflow.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: HOME rent, income, affordability-period, unit-designation, owner reporting, and PJ approval controls
used_for: annual rent and occupancy report scenario design

authoritative_sources.title: 24 CFR 92.508 - Recordkeeping
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: HUD's HOME recordkeeping rule is authoritative for HOME records that participating jurisdictions and owners must retain to support eligibility, rent, occupancy, inspections, fair housing, and project compliance.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: HOME record retention, source-of-truth discipline, monitoring evidence, and auditability controls
used_for: HOME file-readiness and monitoring packet design
refusal when records are unavailable or untraceable

authoritative_sources.title: 26 CFR 1.42-5 - Monitoring compliance with low-income housing credit requirements
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Treasury's Section 42 compliance monitoring rule is authoritative for state housing credit agency monitoring, annual owner certifications, inspections, tenant-record reviews, and noncompliance reporting expectations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: LIHTC compliance monitoring, annual certification, tenant-record review, physical inspection, and noncompliance reporting controls

authoritative_sources.title: Guide for Completing Form 8823, Low-Income Housing Credit Agencies Report of Noncompliance or Building Disposition
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The IRS administers LIHTC federal noncompliance reporting and the Form 8823 guide provides the public issue-category and practical evidence reference used by housing credit agencies.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: LIHTC noncompliance issue classification, correction evidence, owner response support, and tax-credit counsel escalation

authoritative_sources.title: Tenant Rental Assistance Certification System (TRACS)
authoritative_sources.publisher: U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: HUD TRACS is a HUD multifamily subsidy workflow and system of record; HUD's TRACS public page defines the assisted multifamily certification and voucher workflow area and points materializers to current TRACS operating materials.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: HUD-assisted tenant certification transmission, voucher evidence, certification date reconciliation, and system discrepancy handling

authoritative_sources.title: HUD Income Limits
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: HUD publishes income limits used across affordable housing programs and these public data tables are authoritative for AMI-based eligibility when the program, location, bedroom or household size, and effective year are known.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Income-limit source selection, tenant eligibility support, annual source refresh, and stale-source detection
used_for: source refresh and stale evidence scenarios

authoritative_sources.title: HOME Rent Limits
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: HUD Exchange publishes HOME rent limits and tools used by participating jurisdictions and owners to evaluate HOME rent compliance, making it authoritative for HOME rent-limit evidence.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: HOME rent-limit refresh, rent and utility allowance review, and owner/PJ rent approval support
used_for: current source and utility allowance prerequisites

authoritative_sources.title: Disability Rights in Housing
authoritative_sources.publisher: U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: HUD's Office of Fair Housing and Equal Opportunity enforces disability-related civil rights in housing and this official HUD page constrains accommodation, modification, accessibility, and fair housing escalation behavior.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Reasonable accommodation evidence handling, fair housing routing, and civil-rights boundary escalation
used_for: refusal of discrimination or accommodation determinations

workflow_stages.name: Request intake and program-stack grounding
workflow_stages.description: Capture request intent, tenant policy hierarchy, jurisdiction, property, project, building, BIN, unit, household, certification date, program stack, funding source, regulatory agreement, HOME written agreement, HAP or subsidy contract, investor, lender, agency owner, systems of record, and approval matrix before producing compliance outputs.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Obligation, policy, and control mapping
workflow_stages.description: Map HUD multifamily, HOME, LIHTC Section 42, state HFA, PJ, fair housing, regulatory agreement, investor, lender, and tenant obligations to controls, evidence artifacts, evidence owners, review cadence, and escalation triggers.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Affordable housing evidence assembly and source-of-truth review
workflow_stages.description: Validate tenant selection plan, waitlist, application, TIC, income and asset verification, EIV/TRACS evidence, lease, rent roll, utility allowance, subsidy, unit designation, HOME rent and occupancy report, owner certification, inspection records, fair housing records, agency correspondence, and remediation artifacts against named systems of record.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Finding classification and boundary triage
workflow_stages.description: Classify findings as missing file evidence, stale limits, rent or utility allowance issue, income-certification gap, over-income or unit-mix risk, EIV/TRACS discrepancy, HOME fixed/floating conflict, fair housing escalation, property standards concern, Form 8823 risk, or legal/tax/agency/financial/safety authority matter.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Remediation tracking and review preparation
workflow_stages.description: Prepare evidence packets, monitoring-readiness summaries, obligation matrices, exception notes, remediation owner trackers, file correction checklists, agency response support, and assumption logs without claiming final compliance, legal, tax, investor, fair housing, agency, or safety signoff.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Escalation, refusal, and adjacent-owner handoff
workflow_stages.description: Refuse or escalate legal interpretation, tax-credit recapture or reporting strategy, Form 8823 legal conclusions, agency negotiation, fair housing decisions, reasonable accommodation approvals or denials, tenant rights, eviction questions, rent approval, financial approvals, investor or lender waivers, and property safety release while preserving evidence for authorized owners.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

artifacts: Regulatory agreements, extended use agreements, HOME written agreements, HAP contracts, subsidy contracts, investor requirements, lender covenants, management agreements, tenant policies, approval matrices, compliance calendars, and agency monitoring notices

Tenant selection plan, affirmative fair housing marketing plan, waitlist records, applications, eligibility worksheets, household composition records, student status records, citizenship or consent forms where applicable, TICs, income and asset verifications, EIV income and identity reports, and tenant file checklists

Leases, lease addenda, rent rolls, gross rent calculations, utility allowance schedules, income limit tables, rent limit tables, subsidy records, concessions, rent ledgers, notices, unit transfer records, over-income or next-available-unit evidence, and HOME fixed/floating unit designation records

LIHTC annual owner certifications, state HFA portal records, monitoring findings, Form 8823 issue support, correction evidence, owner responses, investor compliance reports, and recertification trackers

HOME rent and occupancy reports, PJ approval records, IDIS or PJ portal references, inspection records, property standards evidence, financial oversight evidence where relevant, and period-of-affordability records
Fair housing, Section 504, reasonable accommodation, accessibility, grievance, resident communication, language access, privacy, retention, legal-hold, and agency correspondence records
systems_of_record: State housing finance agency compliance portal
Accounting ledger, rent ledger, and subsidy receivable system
Fair housing, reasonable accommodation, or resident services case system
Asset management, investor reporting, lender reporting, and development document repositories
Agency correspondence and remediation tracking system

allowed: Classify affordable housing compliance requests as in scope, out of scope, or blocked on missing prerequisites.
Map obligations to controls, evidence owners, review cadence, and systems of record.
Determine whether visible evidence is complete, missing, stale, conflicting, untraceable, or blocked by required owner review.
Prepare tenant-file, unit, rent, income, EIV/TRACS, HOME, LIHTC, fair housing, inspection, monitoring, and remediation evidence packets for authorized owners.
escalate: Tenant policy, jurisdiction, property, program stack, regulatory agreement, HOME written agreement, HAP contract, state HFA rule, investor requirement, lender covenant, unit designation, household facts, certification date, income limit, rent limit, utility allowance, or system-of-record evidence is missing.
HUD, IRS, HOME, state HFA, tenant policy, investor, lender, local rule, fair housing source, operational practice, or agency monitoring notice conflict without an approved precedence path.

Legal, tax-credit counsel, fair housing officer, regulator-facing, agency monitor, investor, lender, property management, leasing, resident services, finance, development, maintenance, accessibility, environmental, or safety authority is required.
Operational practice conflicts with tenant selection plan, waitlist procedure, fair housing requirements, rent approval workflow, tenant file controls, or program source records.
refuse: Request seeks legal advice, tax-credit recapture or reporting strategy, Form 8823 legal conclusion, regulator or agency negotiation, formal certification, or final compliance signoff.
Request asks the lane to approve or deny a reasonable accommodation, decide discrimination risk, determine tenant rights, approve eviction strategy, or adjudicate a grievance.
Request asks the lane to approve rent increases, concessions, reserve draws, investor waivers, lender consents, development budgets, or financial remedies.
Request asks the lane to clear property occupancy, habitability, accessibility, environmental condition, mold, life safety, or licensed inspection findings.

domain_failure_modes: Producing a generic compliance checklist that ignores affordable housing records such as regulatory agreements, extended use agreements, HOME written agreements, HAP contracts, TICs, income and asset verifications, EIV/TRACS artifacts, rent/income limit tables, utility allowances, waitlists, unit designations, owner certifications, and monitoring notices.

Closing a tenant file or monitoring finding when income, asset, lease, rent, utility allowance, EIV/TRACS, HOME, LIHTC, fair housing, or inspection evidence is incomplete, stale, or not tied to a system of record.
Treating HOME, LIHTC, HUD-assisted, local soft-debt, and investor restrictions as interchangeable rather than layered obligations with different evidence and authority owners.
Using expired income limits, rent limits, or utility allowances when current published sources or tenant-authorized schedules are required.

Resolving conflicts among HUD guidance, IRS Section 42 requirements, HOME rules, state HFA manuals, regulatory agreements, investor requirements, lender covenants, tenant policy, and operational practice without authorized precedence.
Providing legal, tax-credit, fair housing, agency, investor, lender, financial, or regulator-facing conclusions outside lane authority.
Misclassifying property standards, accessibility, environmental, habitability, or life-safety signals as routine paperwork gaps and failing to route to licensed or safety owners.

Assuming one affordable housing baseline applies equally across states, program stacks, property types, set-aside elections, unit designations, subsidy contracts, investors, lenders, and local covenants without tenant-specific facts.

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Boundary case that would overgeneralize from the industry name
Request that requires a more specialized adjacent lane
Missing evidence for a required control or obligation
Request for legal interpretation, regulator negotiation, or formal signoff
A required control is missing evidence
Request for formal legal interpretation or signoff outside lane authority
Scenario proving escalation when industry-specific authority or safety facts are missing
Scenario proving the lane refuses or escalates when: do not treat the industry name as cosmetic; it must materially change the pack
Normal in-scope execution using realistic industry terminology and artifact shapes
Boundary rejection when the request should move to an adjacent specialist
Low-confidence handling when required facts, records, or authority sources are missing
Conflicting-source or conflicting-policy handling that requires escalation instead of confident synthesis
Tenant-specific adaptation handling that preserves the public baseline and records assumptions

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Tenant-specific program stack and source precedence
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: Affordable housing obligations vary by jurisdiction, funding source, state HFA, regulatory agreement, HOME written agreement, HAP contract, investor, lender, and tenant policy. The pack is validated because it requires runtime retrieval and escalation rather than guessing.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: HOTMA, state HFA, and local implementation timing
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: Income and asset calculation implementation may differ by program, tenant policy, and agency guidance. The lane requires current tenant and agency sources before case-specific calculations or conclusions.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Legal, tax, fair housing, agency, and property-safety determinations
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: The lane may identify evidence gaps and route issues, but authorized legal, tax-credit, fair housing, agency, investor, lender, maintenance, inspection, or safety owners decide final determinations.

required_workflow_questions.question: What named-industry records and systems actually govern the lane?
required_workflow_questions.answer: Regulatory agreements, extended use agreements, HOME written agreements, HAP contracts, subsidy contracts, state HFA rules, PJ requirements, investor and lender covenants, tenant selection plans, waitlists, TICs, income/asset verification, leases, rent rolls, utility allowances, income/rent limits, EIV/TRACS reports, owner certifications, HOME rent and occupancy reports, inspection records, fair housing records, state HFA portals, HUD TRACS/iMAX, EIV, IDIS/PJ portals, property management systems, compliance software, accounting ledgers, and document repositories govern the truth set.

required_workflow_questions.question: What failure modes make this industry materially different from a generic function?
required_workflow_questions.answer: Layered LIHTC, HOME, HUD subsidy, state HFA, local soft-debt, investor, lender, and tenant obligations can apply to the same project or unit; stale rent/income limits and utility allowances can change compliance; tenant-file evidence must support eligibility and rent; EIV/TRACS and agency portals create named system dependencies; and fair housing, property standards, tax-credit, agency, and tenant-rights boundaries require specialized escalation.

required_workflow_questions.question: What adjacent authority boundaries constrain the lane?
required_workflow_questions.answer: The lane stops at evidence handling, gap classification, control mapping, review preparation, and escalation. Legal owners decide legal interpretation and regulator strategy; tax-credit counsel and agency owners decide Section 42 reporting and tax consequences; fair housing owners decide accommodation, discrimination, and grievance outcomes; investor/lender/finance owners approve financial changes; property management and leasing owners execute tenant operations; maintenance, accessibility, environmental, and licensed inspection owners decide safety and property-condition release.
